name: gunicorn

metadata.name: gunicorn
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules

documentation:
# Gunicorn

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/gunicorn.svg?style=flat
:alt: PyPI version
:target: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/gunicorn
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/gunicorn.svg
:alt: Supported Python versions
:target: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/gunicorn
```

```{image} https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/actions/workflows/tox.yml/badge.svg
:alt: Build Status
:target: https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/actions/workflows/tox.yml
```

```{image} https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/actions/workflows/lint.yml/badge.svg
:alt: Lint Status
:target: https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/actions/workflows/lint.yml
```

Gunicorn 'Green Unicorn' is a Python WSGI HTTP Server for UNIX. It's a pre-fork
worker model ported from Ruby's [Unicorn] project. The Gunicorn server is broadly
compatible with various web frameworks, simply implemented, light on server
resource usage, and fairly speedy.

Feel free to join us in [#gunicorn] on [Libera.chat].

# Documentation

The documentation is hosted at <https://docs.gunicorn.org>.

# Installation

Gunicorn requires **Python 3.x >= 3.7**.

Install from PyPI:

```
$ pip install gunicorn
```

# Usage

Basic usage:

```
$ gunicorn [OPTIONS] APP_MODULE
```

Where `APP_MODULE` is of the pattern `$(MODULE_NAME):$(VARIABLE_NAME)`. The
module name can be a full dotted path. The variable name refers to a WSGI
callable that should be found in the specified module.

Example with test app:

```
$ cd examples
$ gunicorn --workers=2 test:app
```

# Contributing

See [our complete contributor's guide](CONTRIBUTING.md) for more details.

# License

Gunicorn is released under the MIT License. See the [LICENSE] file for more
details.

[#gunicorn]: https://web.libera.chat/?channels=#gunicorn
[libera.chat]: https://libera.chat/
[license]: https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/blob/master/LICENSE
[unicorn]: https://bogomips.org/unicorn/

A Paste Deployment server runner.

Example configuration:

    [server:main]
    use = egg:gunicorn#main
    host = 127.0.0.1
    port = 5000

modules.name: gunicorn.errors
documentation: This file is part of gunicorn released under the MIT license.
See the NOTICE for more information.
We don't need to call super() in __init__ methods of our
BaseException and Exception classes because we also define
our own __str__ methods so there is no need to pass 'message'
to the base class to get a meaningful output from 'str(exc)'.
pylint: disable=super-init-not-called
we inherit from BaseException here to make sure to not be caught
at application level

modules.name: gunicorn.systemd
documentation: This file is part of gunicorn released under the MIT license.
See the NOTICE for more information.

functions.name: gunicorn.systemd.listen_fds
documentation:
Get the number of sockets inherited from systemd socket activation.

```
param unset_environment

clear systemd environment variables unless False

type unset_environment

bool

return

the number of sockets to inherit from systemd socket activation

rtype

int
```

Returns zero immediately if \$LISTEN_PID is not set to the current pid.
Otherwise, returns the number of systemd activation sockets specified by
\$LISTEN_FDS.

When \$LISTEN_PID matches the current pid, unsets the environment variables
unless the `unset_environment` flag is `False`.

:::{note}
Unlike the sd_listen_fds C function, this implementation does not set
the FD_CLOEXEC flag because the gunicorn arbiter never needs to do this.
:::

:::{seealso}
[https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/sd_listen_fds.html](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/sd_listen_fds.html)
:::

functions.name: gunicorn.systemd.sd_notify
documentation:
Send a notification to systemd. state is a string; see
the man page of sd_notify (http://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/sd_notify.html)
for a description of the allowable values.

If the unset_environment parameter is True, sd_notify() will unset
the $NOTIFY_SOCKET environment variable before returning (regardless of
whether the function call itself succeeded or not). Further calls to
sd_notify() will then fail, but the variable is no longer inherited by
child processes.

functions.name: gunicorn.workers.geventlet._eventlet_serve
documentation:
Serve requests forever.

This code is nearly identical to `eventlet.convenience.serve` except
that it attempts to join the pool at the end, which allows for gunicorn
graceful shutdowns.

functions.name: gunicorn.workers.geventlet._eventlet_stop
documentation:
Stop a greenlet handling a request and close its connection.

This code is lifted from eventlet so as not to depend on undocumented
functions in the library.

functions.name: gunicorn.sock.create_sockets
documentation:
Create a new socket for the configured addresses or file descriptors.

If a configured address is a tuple then a TCP socket is created.
If it is a string, a Unix socket is created. Otherwise, a TypeError is
raised.

modules.name: gunicorn.workers.gthread
documentation: This file is part of gunicorn released under the MIT license.
See the NOTICE for more information.
design:
A threaded worker accepts connections in the main loop, accepted
connections are added to the thread pool as a connection job.
Keepalive connections are put back in the loop waiting for an event.
If no event happen after the keep alive timeout, the connection is
closed.
pylint: disable=no-else-break

methods.name: gunicorn.workers.gthread.ThreadWorker.get_thread_pool
documentation: Override this method to customize how the thread pool is created

See the NOTICE for more information.

classes.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.start
documentation: Initialize the arbiter. Start listening and set pidfile if needed.

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.init_signals
documentation:
Initialize master signal handling. Most of the signals
are queued. Child signals only wake up the master.

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.handle_hup
documentation:
HUP handling.
- Reload configuration
- Start the new worker processes with a new configuration
- Gracefully shutdown the old worker processes

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.handle_ttin
documentation:
SIGTTIN handling.
Increases the number of workers by one.

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.handle_ttou
documentation:
SIGTTOU handling.
Decreases the number of workers by one.

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.handle_usr1
documentation:
SIGUSR1 handling.
Kill all workers by sending them a SIGUSR1

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.handle_usr2
documentation:
SIGUSR2 handling.
Creates a new arbiter/worker set as a fork of the current
arbiter without affecting old workers. Use this to do live
deployment with the ability to backout a change.

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.handle_winch
documentation: SIGWINCH handling

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.wakeup
documentation: Wake up the arbiter by writing to the PIPE

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.halt
documentation: halt arbiter

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.sleep
documentation:
Sleep until PIPE is readable or we timeout.
A readable PIPE means a signal occurred.

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.stop
documentation:
Stop workers

:attr graceful: boolean, If True (the default) workers will be
killed gracefully  (ie. trying to wait for the current connection)

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.reexec
documentation: Relaunch the master and workers.

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.murder_workers
documentation: Kill unused/idle workers

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.reap_workers
documentation: Reap workers to avoid zombie processes

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.manage_workers
documentation:
Maintain the number of workers by spawning or killing
as required.

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.spawn_workers
documentation:
Spawn new workers as needed.

This is where a worker process leaves the main loop
of the master process.

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.kill_workers
documentation:
Kill all workers with the signal `sig`
:attr sig: `signal.SIG*` value

methods.name: gunicorn.arbiter.Arbiter.kill_worker
documentation:
Kill a worker

:attr pid: int, worker pid
:attr sig: `signal.SIG*` value

Arbiter maintain the workers processes alive. It launches or
kills them if needed. It also manages application reloading
via SIGHUP/USR2.

modules.name: gunicorn.workers.base
documentation: This file is part of gunicorn released under the MIT license.
See the NOTICE for more information.

methods.name: gunicorn.workers.base.Worker.__init__
documentation:
This is called pre-fork so it shouldn't do anything to the
current process. If there's a need to make process wide
changes you'll want to do that in `self.init_process()`.

methods.name: gunicorn.workers.base.Worker.notify
documentation:
Your worker subclass must arrange to have this method called
once every `self.timeout` seconds. If you fail in accomplishing
this task, the master process will murder your workers.

methods.name: gunicorn.workers.base.Worker.run
documentation:
This is the mainloop of a worker process. You should override
this method in a subclass to provide the intended behaviour
for your particular evil schemes.

methods.name: gunicorn.workers.base.Worker.init_process
documentation:
If you override this method in a subclass, the last statement
in the function should be to call this method with
super().init_process() so that the `run()` loop is initiated.
